Gastric bypass or vertical sleeve gastrectomy results in dramatic weight reduction and improvement of metabolic syndrome features
Survival benefit observed with gastric bypass
items.reference: The Metabolic Syndrome · LIFESTYLE (SEE ALSO CHAP. 414) (part 2)

items.title: Guidelines for Statin Use in Patients with Metabolic Syndrome
items.content: The ACC/AHA guidelines recommend specific statin intensities based on LDL cholesterol levels and the presence of type 2 diabetes, rather than metabolic syndrome alone. For patients aged 20–75 years with LDL ≥190 mg/dL, high-intensity statins are advised. Those with type 2 diabetes aged 40–75 should use moderate to high-intensity statins depending on risk assessment.
keyPoints: LDL cholesterol levels ≥190 mg/dL: Use high-intensity statin (e.g., atorvastatin 40–80 mg or rosuvastatin 20–40 mg).
Type 2 diabetes aged 40–75 years: Moderate-intensity statin, consider high-intensity if risk is high.
Metabolic syndrome without diabetes: Use 10-year ASCVD risk estimator; discuss statins for primary prevention if risk ≥7.5% and ≤20%, or elevated lifetime risk in those aged 20–59.
items.reference: The Metabolic Syndrome · LDL CHOLESTEROL (SEE ALSO CHAP. 419)

items.title: Pharmacologic Interventions for Elevated LDL Cholesterol
items.content: When dietary interventions fail to control LDL cholesterol, statins are the first-line pharmacological treatment due to their efficacy in lowering LDL by 15–60%. Ezetimibe is recommended as a second-choice medication, reducing LDL by 15–20%. Bempedoic acid and PCSK9 inhibitors are additional options for patients who require further LDL reduction.
keyPoints: Statins lower LDL cholesterol by 15–60%.
Ezetimibe reduces LDL by 15–20%, well tolerated.
Bempedoic acid (alone or with ezetimibe) can reduce LDL by up to 35%.
PCSK9 inhibitors lower LDL by ~45–60%, reserved for specific cases like familial hypercholesterolemia.
items.reference: The Metabolic Syndrome · LDL CHOLESTEROL (SEE ALSO CHAP. 419)

items.title: When to Treat Elevated Fasting Triglycerides
items.content: According to the 2019 ACC/AHA Cholesterol Guidelines, fasting triglyceride levels greater than 500 mg/dL should be treated to prevent serious hypertriglyceridemia and pancreatitis. Although a level above 150 mg/dL is part of metabolic syndrome criteria, there's no clear evidence that lowering triglycerides below 200 mg/dL reduces ASCVD outcomes.
keyPoints: Fasting triglyceride >500 mg/dL should be treated to prevent serious hypertriglyceridemia and pancreatitis.
A fasting triglyceride value of >150 mg/dL is a component of metabolic syndrome.
Post hoc analyses do not suggest a reduction in primary ASCVD outcomes with triglycerides >200 mg/dL, even with statin therapy.
items.reference: The Metabolic Syndrome · TRIGLYCERIDES (SEE ALSO CHAP. 419)

items.title: Choosing the Right Drug to Lower Triglycerides
items.content: Fibrates like gemfibrozil and fenofibrate are preferred for lowering fasting triglyceride levels by 30–45%. Fenofibrate is preferable when combined with statins metabolized by the CYP3A4 system due to a lower risk of myopathy. Omega-3 fatty acids, particularly high-dose EPA, have been shown in trials like JELIS and REDUCE-IT to reduce triglycerides by ~25–40% without significant drug interactions.
keyPoints: Fibrates can reduce fasting triglyceride levels by 30–45%.
Fenofibrate is preferable over gemfibrozil with statins metabolized by CYP3A4 to avoid myopathy risk.
Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA) lower triglycerides by ~25–40%, as seen in JELIS and REDUCE-IT trials.
items.reference: The Metabolic Syndrome · TRIGLYCERIDES (SEE ALSO CHAP. 419)

items.title: Nicotinic Acid and HDL Cholesterol in Metabolic Syndrome
items.content: Nicotinic acid is the only lipid-modifying agent that predictably raises HDL cholesterol levels by up to 30% above baseline. However, trials have shown no beneficial effect on ASCVD events when used with statins in patients with or without metabolic syndrome.
keyPoints: Statins, fibrates, and bile acid sequestrants modestly increase HDL (5–10%).
Ezetimibe and omega-3 fatty acids have no effect on HDL cholesterol.
Nicotinic acid can raise HDL cholesterol by up to 30% above baseline.
No evidence of beneficial effects on ASCVD events with nicotinic acid in statin-treated patients.

items.title: Management of Hypertension in Metabolic Syndrome
items.content: For patients with metabolic syndrome without diabetes, ACE inhibitors or angiotensin II receptor blockers are preferred initial antihypertensive medications due to their efficacy and tolerability. Lifestyle modifications including a sodium-restricted diet enriched in fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and low-fat dairy products should be recommended.
keyPoints: Blood pressure categories: Hypertension (>140/90 mmHg), Prehypertension (>120/80 mmHg but <140/90 mmHg), Normal (<120/80 mmHg).
ACE inhibitors or angiotensin II receptor blockers are preferred for initial treatment in metabolic syndrome without diabetes.
Additional antihypertensive options include diuretics, calcium channel blockers, beta blockers, and mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists like finerenone.
Advocate sodium-restricted diet enriched with fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and low-fat dairy products.
items.reference: The Metabolic Syndrome · HDL CHOLESTEROL (SEE ALSO CHAP. 419) | BLOOD PRESSURE (SEE ALSO CHAP. 288) | IMPAIRED FASTING GLUCOSE (SEE ALSO CHAP. 415)

items.title: Mechanisms and Effects of Drugs on Insulin Resistance
items.content: Biguanides like metformin and thiazolidinediones (TZDs) such as pioglitazone increase insulin sensitivity by enhancing insulin action in the liver and suppressing endogenous glucose production. TZDs also improve insulin-mediated glucose uptake in muscle and adipose tissue, unlike metformin.
keyPoints: Metformin enhances insulin action in the liver and suppresses endogenous glucose production.
TZDs enhance insulin action in the liver and improve insulin-mediated glucose uptake in muscle and adipose tissue.
Pioglitazone associated with reduction in ASCVD events in patients with metabolic syndrome, prediabetes, and type 2 diabetes (meta-analysis of nine trials involving 12,026 participants).
Adverse effects of TZDs include weight gain, bone fracture, and congestive heart failure with/or without edema.
items.reference: The Metabolic Syndrome · INSULIN RESISTANCE (SEE ALSO CHAP. 416)

items.title: Clinical Benefits and Risks of TZDs in Insulin Resistance
items.content: TZDs like pioglitazone reduce ASCVD events in patients with insulin resistance, prediabetes, and type 2 diabetes. They also benefit patients with MAFLD but have adverse effects such as weight gain, bone fracture, and congestive heart failure.
keyPoints: Pioglitazone reduces ASCVD events in patients with metabolic syndrome, prediabetes, and type 2 diabetes (meta-analysis of nine trials involving 12,026 participants).
Benefit of TZDs seen in patients with MAFLD.
Adverse effects include weight gain, bone fracture, and congestive heart failure with/or without edema.
items.reference: The Metabolic Syndrome · INSULIN RESISTANCE (SEE ALSO CHAP. 416)

items.statement: A ~500-kcal restriction daily equates to weight reduction of approximately 2 lbs per week.
items.explanation: ~500 kcal/day restriction equates to weight reduction of 1 lb per week, not 2 lbs.
items.reference: The Metabolic Syndrome · DIAGNOSIS | LIFESTYLE (SEE ALSO CHAP. 414) (part 1)

items.statement: High-dose liraglutide is contraindicated for patients with uncontrolled hypertension.
items.explanation: Naltrexone/bupropion, not high-dose liraglutide, should not be given to patients with uncontrolled hypertension.
items.reference: The Metabolic Syndrome · LIFESTYLE (SEE ALSO CHAP. 414) (part 2)

items.statement: Metabolic surgery is recommended for patients with a BMI >30 kg/m^2 and type 2 diabetes.
items.explanation: An evolving application for metabolic surgery includes patients with a BMI as low as 30 kg/m^2 and type 2 diabetes.
items.reference: The Metabolic Syndrome · LIFESTYLE (SEE ALSO CHAP. 414) (part 2)

items.statement: For patients with metabolic syndrome but without diabetes, statin therapy should be initiated if their 10-year ASCVD risk is ≥7.5% and ≤20%.
items.explanation: Patients with a 10-year ASCVD risk of ≥7.5% and ≤20% or those aged 20–59 with elevated lifetime risk should discuss statin therapy initiation.
items.reference: The Metabolic Syndrome · LDL CHOLESTEROL (SEE ALSO CHAP. 419)

items.statement: PCSK9 inhibitors are the first-choice medication for most patients with metabolic syndrome.
items.explanation: PCSK9 inhibitors are not needed for most patients with metabolic syndrome; statins are the first choice.
items.reference: The Metabolic Syndrome · LDL CHOLESTEROL (SEE ALSO CHAP. 419)

items.statement: The degree of triglyceride lowering in fibrate trials predicts benefit.
items.explanation: The degree of triglyceride lowering did not predict benefit.
items.reference: The Metabolic Syndrome · TRIGLYCERIDES (SEE ALSO CHAP. 419)

items.statement: Prescriptions of omega-3 fatty acid preparations can lower fasting triglyceride levels by ~25–40%.
items.explanation: Omega-3 fatty acids can indeed lower fasting triglyceride levels by this amount.
items.reference: The Metabolic Syndrome · TRIGLYCERIDES (SEE ALSO CHAP. 419)

items.subtopic: HDL CHOLESTEROL (SEE ALSO CHAP. 419) | BLOOD PRESSURE (SEE ALSO CHAP. 288) | IMPAIRED FASTING GLUCOSE (SEE ALSO CHAP. 415)
items.statement: Statins have a significant effect on raising HDL cholesterol levels.
items.explanation: Statins have modest effects on HDL cholesterol, increasing it by only 5–10%.
items.reference: The Metabolic Syndrome · HDL CHOLESTEROL (SEE ALSO CHAP. 419) | BLOOD PRESSURE (SEE ALSO CHAP. 288) | IMPAIRED FASTING GLUCOSE (SEE ALSO CHAP. 415)

items.subtopic: HDL CHOLESTEROL (SEE ALSO CHAP. 419) | BLOOD PRESSURE (SEE ALSO CHAP. 288) | IMPAIRED FASTING GLUCOSE (SEE ALSO CHAP. 415)
items.statement: Aggressive glycemic control in patients with metabolic syndrome and type 2 diabetes can modify fasting levels of triglycerides and/or HDL cholesterol.
items.explanation: Aggressive glycemic control is noted to favorably modify these lipid levels.
items.reference: The Metabolic Syndrome · HDL CHOLESTEROL (SEE ALSO CHAP. 419) | BLOOD PRESSURE (SEE ALSO CHAP. 288) | IMPAIRED FASTING GLUCOSE (SEE ALSO CHAP. 415)

items.statement: Metformin enhances insulin action in muscle and adipose tissue.
items.explanation: Metformin enhances insulin action primarily in the liver, not in muscle and adipose tissue.
items.reference: The Metabolic Syndrome · INSULIN RESISTANCE (SEE ALSO CHAP. 416)

items.statement: TZDs have been associated with a reduction in ASCVD events in patients with metabolic syndrome.
items.explanation: Pioglitazone, a TZD, was shown to reduce ASCVD events in patients with metabolic syndrome.
items.reference: The Metabolic Syndrome · INSULIN RESISTANCE (SEE ALSO CHAP. 416)

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS | Three or more of the following:
items.assertion: The prevalence of metabolic syndrome increases with age.
items.reason: Nearly 50% of the U.S. population aged >60 has metabolic syndrome.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: The Metabolic Syndrome · RISK FACTORS | Three or more of the following:

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS | Three or more of the following:
items.assertion: Metabolic syndrome is solely determined by obesity.
items.reason: Central adiposity is a key feature of metabolic syndrome.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because metabolic syndrome involves multiple factors, not just obesity.
items.reference: The Metabolic Syndrome · RISK FACTORS | Three or more of the following:

items.assertion: Increased waist circumference is associated with greater prevalence of metabolic syndrome in Asians and Asian Indians.
items.reason: Relative increases in visceral versus subcutaneous adipose tissue occur with increasing waist circumference in these populations.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: The Metabolic Syndrome · ETIOLOGY (part 1)

items.assertion: Hypertriglyceridemia is an excellent marker of insulin resistance.
items.reason: It results from increased production of apoB-containing VLDLs due to free fatty acid flux from adipose tissue to the liver.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: The Metabolic Syndrome · ETIOLOGY (part 1)

items.assertion: Adiponectin inhibits gluconeogenesis and enhances fatty acid oxidation.
items.reason: This occurs through the activation of AMP kinase in muscle tissue.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: The Metabolic Syndrome · ETIOLOGY (part 2)

items.assertion: Insulin resistance is solely responsible for hypertension in metabolic syndrome.
items.reason: This is due to increased activity of the sympathetic nervous system and sodium reabsorption.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as insulin resistance contributes only partially, not solely.
items.reference: The Metabolic Syndrome · ETIOLOGY (part 2)

items.assertion: Obstructive sleep apnea may predict metabolic syndrome.
items.reason: Obstructive sleep apnea is associated with obesity, hypertension, and insulin resistance.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the presence of obstructive sleep apnea can indeed predict metabolic syndrome due to its association with related conditions.
items.reference: The Metabolic Syndrome · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.assertion: The risk for type 2 diabetes is increased three- to fivefold in patients with metabolic syndrome.
items.reason: This increase is primarily due to elevated fasting plasma glucose levels.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, as increases in fasting plasma glucose explain most of the increased risk for type 2 diabetes among patients with metabolic syndrome.
items.reference: The Metabolic Syndrome · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.assertion: Weight regains after successful weight reduction underscore the need for long-lasting behavioral changes.
items.reason: Behavioral treatment includes dietary restriction and increased physical activity to achieve sufficient weight loss that benefits metabolic health.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why long-lasting behavioral changes are necessary.
items.reference: The Metabolic Syndrome · DIAGNOSIS | LIFESTYLE (SEE ALSO CHAP. 414) (part 1)

items.assertion: A diet restricted in carbohydrate typically provides a more rapid initial weight loss.
items.reason: After one year, the amount of weight reduction with low-carbohydrate diets is significantly greater than that with caloric restriction alone.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not correctly explain the assertion as both diet types result in similar long-term weight loss.
items.reference: The Metabolic Syndrome · DIAGNOSIS | LIFESTYLE (SEE ALSO CHAP. 414) (part 1)
